paragraphs.body: The article “Who Were the Celts?” written or edited by the HISTORY.com editors Amanda Onion, Missy Sullivan, Matt Mullen, and Christian Zapata, provides an insightful overview of the Celts and their lasting impact on modern societies. It traces the origins of the Celts as a diverse group of tribes that spread across Europe, known for their rich culture, famous artistry, and warrior spirit. This topic is important to me because of its relationship that I share with being Scottish and Irish. An ancestry that has shaped my life in many ways.

annotations.text: This topic is important to me because of its relationship that I share with being Scottish and Irish. An ancestry that has shaped my life in many ways.
annotations.comment: Remember that summary is all "they say." There's no "I say." :) Let's talk about the "art" of summary and how you might "point" your reader to your own essay.

paragraphs.body: The Celts were a group of tribes with origins in central Europe. They shared similar languages, religions, and traditions. The Celtic people spread throughout Western Europe in places like Ireland, France, Britain, and Spain because of their migrations. Many of the Celtic traditions and languages are still prominent in parts of the world today.

The Celtic people and culture are much more varied and widespread than most people know. Celtic culture crossed many land masses and societies, all sharing common Celtic roots. “Several tribes made up the larger population of the Celtic people. Indeed, the Gaels, Gauls, Britons, Irish, and Galatians were all Celtic tribes.” Their culture and traditions ranged across the European continent, spreading far from the British Isles where they’re generally thought to be confined.

annotations.comment: So much interesting info, here! But see how this whole page is summary? How much of this summary is actually necessary to "point"? Otherwise, you run the risk of writing a report rather than an essay based on your own agenda :)
annotations.context_left: Celtic roots. “Several tribes made up the larger population of the Celtic people. Indeed,

paragraphs.body: The Roman Empire called Celts “Galli,” which meant barbarians. "However, the Celts… were anything but barbarians.” This is proven by the large amount of culture that has been passed down through generations. The Roman Empire, the same people who called the Celts barbarians, launched a campaign against the Celtic people killing thousands and utterly destroying their culture in Europe. The Roman Empire was not fully successful in their campaign, so the Celtic people established a homeland in the area of modern-day Britain. And because of that, many cultural traditions are still popular now in Wales, Ireland, and Scotland.

Many languages trace their origins to the Celtic times. The native tongue in Wales is Welsh and is taught still today. A small percentage of the Cornwall population speak Cornish. In Scotland and Ireland, the Celtic language that is still spoken today by a small amount of people is Scotts Gaelic or Irish Gaelic. Many of these languages are slowly being forgotten due to the lack of their appearance and use in today's society.

This article also shares information about the Celt’s music and their famous artistic designs. Music like Scottish bagpipes can be traced back to Celtic times. Even now, intricate stone carvings and metalwork using gold, silver, and other valuable jewels are produced. These items are now not made simply for representation and expression of culture, but also because many people, including those without Celtic ancestry, find the popular design appealing.

annotations.comment: Still report and summary, all the way to this paragraph.
annotations.context_left: now, intricate stone carvings and metalwork using gold, silver, and other valuable jewels

paragraphs.body: My family enjoys shopping at the Irish store on Craig Street in Oakland for plaid and other Irish-related stuff. This is another example of how small items are important to my family. I lost a close family member this summer. It was not a surprise, and we had been prepared for it for a long time. His death was followed by numerous Irish celebrations, such as our Irish wake and the songs we sang, all of which were tied to him and meaningful for our family.

annotations.text: This is another example of how small items are important to my family. I lost a close family member this summer. It was not a surprise, and we had been prepared for it for a long time.

paragraphs.body: My grandmother, Percilla McNulty, known to everyone as Kitsy, has always expressed our family’s heritage in various ways. After my mom's father passed away, she replaced her wedding ring with an Irish Celtic knot ring as a tribute to her roots. Beyond her fondness for plaid blankets and scarves, the most cherished memories are of her singing voice. When I was younger, my grandmother would sing me lullabies in Irish Gaelic. Those memories I hold close to me.

annotations.text: When I was younger, my grandmother would sing me lullabies in Irish Gaelic.

paragraphs.body: I've been surrounded by the influences Celtic culture my entire life, yet I’ve never set foot in Scotland or Ireland. I’ve never spoken the languages or cheered for one of their sports teams. Does that make me any less Scottish or Irish? While it’s true that there is a separation between me and those who live in Scotland and Ireland, their traditions and small things like rings are part of why I am who I am today.

annotations.text: of why I am who I am today.
annotations.comment: The question is, of course: Who _are_ you? specifically, I mean, because of your Irish heritage?

paragraphs.body: Even though I've never been to Ireland and Scotland, I believe my family's ties to the country make me feel as though it does not matter, but are my family ties strong enough to fully connect me to my Scottish and Irish heritage? I consider myself to be Irish and Scottish, but I'm not sure if people who live in Irland and Scotland will think the same. I am after all a regular American; however, I have a deeper connection to something. My family does have a connection to this, but do I qualify to be Irish and Scottish?

annotations.text: have a deeper connection to something.

paragraphs.body: I am not a respected warrior like the ancient Celts. I have none of the language skills, and I have no idea how to carve beautiful designs into stones, or work magic with silver and gold. Ultimately, I am an American, regardless of my Scottish and Irish heritage. That's who I am, despite how much I sometimes despise America, and the Irish and Scottish cultures may have an effect on my family. It doesn't change that I am still an American. I may be both American, Scottish, and Irish, and while some of these things have had a greater impact on my life than others, I should be able to acknowledge and cherish those aspects of myself.

annotations.text: how much I sometimes despise America,
I should be able to acknowledge and cherish those aspects of myself.
annotations.comment: Yes. But which specifically? And why? Let's talk :)

paragraphs.body: Who were the Celts? The Celts were a group of talented peoples who, through migration, spread across many parts of Europe and thrived. Their influence continues to echo around the world today. My family is an example of those impacted by the ancient Celts, though we may not fully embody what it means to be Irish or Scottish in America. Despite our connections to certain traditions, there are others with deeper connections. Nevertheless, these traditions are significant to me, reflecting centuries of migration and development by the Celts. The article "Who Were the Celts?" written by the editors of History.com provides valuable insight into how our relationships with the past can shape our future.

Work Cited: https://www.history.com/topics/european-history/celts Sources my source used: Who were the ancient Celts? Shoreline Community College.
